over(^) leaves an extra copy so the tail reduces to a single value; swap(~) leaves one fewer value, so a later '+' underflows. Halt->fault flip from a one-keystroke glyph.
operands of the non-commutative subtraction swapped; both compile and run.
div and mod look almost identical and only diverge for this specific dividend/divisor pair; the rest of the chain preserves the small delta to the final value.
the boundary case i==n flips whether it is counted; one extra term.
identical control: a dense mixed +-*/% chain with no combinators, so 'no diff' must be confirmed glyph by glyph.
identical control.
identical control containing a quote+apply, so a reader may wrongly suspect the quote hides a change.
identical control with a nested helper function.
rot(@) consumes three values and reduces to one; over(^) copies instead of rotating, leaving an extra value that shifts the whole tail. Buried two quotes deep.
the single use of the variable 'e' is renamed to 'd', silently reusing the wrong binding.
dip(') shields the value beneath the quote; apply(!) runs the quote directly on it. ' and ! differ by one keystroke and both are legal here, so no fault to tip the reader off.
and/or differ by two letters and flip which branch runs for mixed-sign inputs.
cat(,) concatenates the two quotes into one runnable body; cons(;) nests the first quote as an element, so applying it later multiplies a quote by an int and faults. Halt->fault from ',' vs ';'.
operands of one subtraction swapped, buried inside a compound expression.
identical control with two levels of nested quotes and two apply sites, maximizing the chance of a false 'they differ'.
identical control with a loop and modulus.
a single interior digit of a long literal changes; the additive/multiplicative tail (no mod) preserves the +80 offset to the final value, but the digit is easy to skim past.
one interior digit of a 4-digit literal changed deep in the body.

dup(:) squares the top each iteration; over(^) multiplies it by the constant beneath instead. Both keep the stack depth stable so the loop runs identically-shaped; only the numbers differ. Swap sits inside a loop body.
the single use of parameter 'c' in the return is renamed to 'a'.

'<' (3<8 -> 1, true branch) vs '=' (3=8 -> 0, false branch) pick different quotes; the long additive tail carries the branch delta to the end. '<' and '=' are a single glyph apart and both yield a valid flag.
the boundary i*2==n case flips inclusion, adding one term deep in the loop.
identical control combining primrec (&) and apply (!) with a long arithmetic tail, so confirming 'no diff' requires reading through a recursion.
identical control with a fibonacci-style loop.

primrec(&) expects n[I][C]; linrec(|) expects [P][T][R1][R2]. Swapping the one glyph feeds primrec's operands (an Int count plus two quotes) to linrec, which finds an Int where the fourth quote must be and faults. The quietest semantic swap: one glyph, halt->fault.
operands of a non-commutative subtraction swapped inside a compound expression.

Deleting one '>' makes B pull only a single element off the list, then '+' underflows across the drained stack; A pulls two elements and adds them. A one-glyph present-vs-absent change buried in a list walk. Halt->fault.
off-by-one: '<=' includes one extra index, mirroring the extra element the MTL uncons consumes.

A single interior digit of the 3-digit literal 137 flips to 139 (137->139), buried 18 glyphs into a 77-glyph arithmetic chain of identical-looking N*M+N-... groups; the surrounding digits camouflage the change and both sides HALT with only the final total differing.

swap (~) becomes over (^) inside the inner quote of a doubly-nested quote [[6 ~-]!...]; over leaves an extra copy so B's stack carries a stray value all the way to HALT, but the single glyph sits two bracket levels deep among visually similar stack ops.

the inner if's predicate 4 6< becomes 4 6= inside the then-branch of an outer if; < is true (4<6) but = is false (4=6), so the two nested branches diverge, yet the changed glyph is one of two adjacent comparison symbols deep inside nested [ ] and ? .

the native fold ( becomes primrec & right after [seq] init [C]; fold expects [seq] init [C] but primrec expects int [I] [C], so B reads the list literal as the recursion count and faults TypeMismatch while A HALTs. Halt->fault from one combinator glyph.
byte-identical 67-glyph program: a fold over a 5-element list followed by a long %-laden arithmetic tail; confirming no change requires scanning every group.
byte-identical 66-glyph program with a nested if and a %-laden tail; the density and bracket nesting make 'no difference' hard to confirm.
one interior digit of the 4-digit literal 1374 flips to 1376 inside an accumulation loop; parallel to the MTL deep-digit swap.
(a - b) becomes (b - a) in the first term of a non-commutative expression summed in a loop; mirrors the MTL swap/over stack change.
loop guard i*2 < n becomes i*2 <= n, an off-by-one that admits one extra index; mirrors the MTL <->= predicate flip.
(b * 3 - a) becomes (a * 3 - b) in the second floor-division term; a quiet operand swap paralleling the MTL fold/primrec divergence.
byte-identical accumulation over a 7-element list; genuinely hard to confirm unchanged.
byte-identical parity-branching loop; no difference to find.
Divides by (n-5); shown n=5 makes the divisor 0 (DivByZero). A shallow reader computes (3n+7)/(n-5)*2+4 as a concrete int; n=8 halts at 24.
Divides by (n-5); shown n=5 makes the divisor 0 (DivByZero). A shallow reader computes (3n+7)/(n-5)*2+4 as a concrete int; n=8 halts at 24.
The if-branch selects 100/0 only when n<5; shown n=2 takes the faulting branch, whereas n>=5 returns 7.
The if-branch selects 100/0 only when n<5; shown n=2 takes the faulting branch, whereas n>=5 returns 7.
Drops from a 4-value stack `k` times; the 5th drop underflows. Shown count 6 overshoots; count<=4 leaves a value (10).
Drops from a 4-value stack `k` times; the 5th drop underflows. Shown count 6 overshoots; count<=4 leaves a value (10).
CONTROL: looks like a divide-by-input hazard, but 200//8 is fine, so it halts at 528; only n=0 would fault (DivByZero).
CONTROL: looks like a divide-by-input hazard, but 200//8 is fine, so it halts at 528; only n=0 would fault (DivByZero).
Left fold divides the accumulator by each element; the 0 element makes acc//0 fault mid-fold. Reader must fold to the 0; a list without 0 halts at 1.
Left fold divides the accumulator by each element; the 0 element makes acc//0 fault mid-fold. Reader must fold to the 0; a list without 0 halts at 1.
Checked i64 product overflows on the 4th factor (900000^4 > 2^63-1); three factors stay in range (7.29e17). Python ints are unbounded, so the twin enforces the i64 bound explicitly.
Checked i64 product overflows on the 4th factor (900000^4 > 2^63-1); three factors stay in range (7.29e17). Python ints are unbounded, so the twin enforces the i64 bound explicitly.
Uncons-and-sum loop runs `k` times over a 5-element list; after it empties, the swap underflows. Shown k=7 overshoots; k=5 sums to 173.
Uncons-and-sum loop runs `k` times over a 5-element list; after it empties, the swap underflows. Shown k=7 overshoots; k=5 sums to 173.
CONTROL: a multi-digit product fold looks overflow-prone but stays under 2^63, halting at 344042899200; larger factors (e.g. 1000000^4) would overflow.
CONTROL: a multi-digit product fold looks overflow-prone but stays under 2^63, halting at 344042899200; larger factors (e.g. 1000000^4) would overflow.
Fold divides acc by (w-7); the divisor hits 0 at the element w=7, ninth in the list. Deep simulation required; with 7 replaced by 10 there is no zero divisor and it halts at 0.
Fold divides acc by (w-7); the divisor hits 0 at the element w=7, ninth in the list. Deep simulation required; with 7 replaced by 10 there is no zero divisor and it halts at 0.
1000^k product overflows i64 at the 7th factor (1000^7 = 1e21); the shown 9-element list faults, six factors give 1e18. Twin enforces the i64 bound to match MTL checked multiply.
1000^k product overflows i64 at the 7th factor (1000^7 = 1e21); the shown 9-element list faults, six factors give 1e18. Twin enforces the i64 bound to match MTL checked multiply.
Summing fold hits a quote element [9] tenth in the list; adding int+quote is a TypeMismatch. Reader must reach it; with 18 in its place the sum is 545.
Summing fold hits a quote element [9] tenth in the list; adding int+quote is a TypeMismatch. Reader must reach it; with 18 in its place the sum is 545.
CONTROL: same divide-fold shape as folddivdeep but with no w=7 element, so it never divides by zero; deep simulation confirms it halts at 0. A w=7 element would fault.
CONTROL: same divide-fold shape as folddivdeep but with no w=7 element, so it never divides by zero; deep simulation confirms it halts at 0. A w=7 element would fault.

Fold divides acc by (w-17); only the LAST (11th) element equals 17, making the divisor 0 (DivByZero). A shallow reader folds a plausible quotient chain and confabulates an int; every element must be simulated to reach the fault. With the last 17 replaced by 24 it halts at 0.

Fold divides acc by (w-17); only the LAST (11th) element equals 17, making the divisor 0 (DivByZero). A shallow reader folds a plausible quotient chain and confabulates an int; every element must be simulated to reach the fault. With the last 17 replaced by 24 it halts at 0.

Checked i64 product of 24 overflows only at the 14th (last, deepest) multiply; the first 13 factors stay in range (24^13 = 876488338465357824). Deep simulation to the final element is required. Python ints are unbounded, so the twin enforces the i64 bound explicitly; the first 13 factors halt at 876488338465357824.

Checked i64 product of 24 overflows only at the 14th (last, deepest) multiply; the first 13 factors stay in range (24^13 = 876488338465357824). Deep simulation to the final element is required. Python ints are unbounded, so the twin enforces the i64 bound explicitly; the first 13 factors halt at 876488338465357824.

Uncons-and-sum times loop runs k=15 times over an 11-element list; after draining all 11 elements the swap underflows on the 12th pop. A shallow reader stops at the running sum; k=11 drains exactly and halts at 571.

Uncons-and-sum times loop runs k=15 times over an 11-element list; after draining all 11 elements the swap underflows on the 12th pop. A shallow reader stops at the running sum; k=11 drains exactly and halts at 571.

Linrec divides the accumulator by each element head-first during descent; the divisor hits 0 only at the deepest recursion level (the last element is 0). The reader must descend the entire list to reach the fault. With the last 0 replaced by 1 it halts at 24801.

Linrec divides the accumulator by each element head-first during descent; the divisor hits 0 only at the deepest recursion level (the last element is 0). The reader must descend the entire list to reach the fault. With the last 0 replaced by 1 it halts at 24801.
CONTROL: a 16-factor product of 15 looks overflow-prone, but 15^16 = 6568408355712890625 stays under 2^63-1, so it halts; a 17th factor would overflow. Punishes over-eager 'it overflows' guessing.
CONTROL: a 16-factor product of 15 looks overflow-prone, but 15^16 = 6568408355712890625 stays under 2^63-1, so it halts; a 17th factor would overflow. Punishes over-eager 'it overflows' guessing.

CONTROL: same linrec divide-walk as d4 with divisors decrementing 9..1 toward zero, but the list contains no 0 element, so it never divides by zero and halts at 2755 after the full recursion. Punishes over-eager 'the divisor hits zero' guessing.

CONTROL: same linrec divide-walk as d4 with divisors decrementing 9..1 toward zero, but the list contains no 0 element, so it never divides by zero and halts at 2755 after the full recursion. Punishes over-eager 'the divisor hits zero' guessing.
